title: Fishing

jokes.text: Dan's motivational speech to new staffers: 'Selina Meyer will never forget you.' Immediately followed by Kent: 'You are going to change America in a way that usually only war and fluoride can.'
jokes.setup: Dan delivers a grandiose, inspirational speech about serving Selina Meyer, setting up an earnest motivational tone.
jokes.punchline: Kent's deadpan addendum equates their campaign work with 'war and fluoride' — two wildly mismatched vectors of American change, delivered with total sincerity.
jokes.explanation: Kent's specificity — not just 'war' but 'war AND fluoride' — is the joke. Fluoride is such a mundane, bureaucratic instrument of national change that pairing it with war creates a perfectly calibrated absurdist deflation. It's entirely Kent's voice: data-obsessed, affectless, weirdly precise. A strong opener that establishes the show's register immediately.

jokes.text: Dan's induction speech: 'Not in a Jewish way, either. In a, like, selected from a very short list of 10 way.'
jokes.setup: Dan calls the new staffers 'the chosen ones,' invoking obvious religious/cultural connotations.
jokes.punchline: He immediately panics at the implication and clarifies it means 'selected from a very short list of 10' — which is itself a deflating anti-compliment.
jokes.explanation: Classic Veep structure: a character tries to sound inspirational, immediately undercuts themselves. The correction is funnier than the original — 'a very short list of 10' means these staffers were barely chosen at all. Dan's social anxiety about ethnic/religious optics is a nice character beat.

jokes.text: Dan: 'Some people say three microwaves is overkill. My response is always, "Tapas."'
jokes.setup: Dan is listing operational campaign details in a breathless induction speech.
jokes.punchline: 'Tapas' as a complete rebuttal to 'three microwaves is overkill' — a non-sequitur that somehow feels like Dan logic.
jokes.explanation: The joke is almost purely structural: the confidence with which Dan deploys 'tapas' as if it's a devastating logical counter. It's completely unexplained and unapologetically absurd. The economy is perfect — one word punchline. Very quotable precisely because it makes no sense.

jokes.text: Selina and team react to Thornhill: 'What is our take on this one-dick pony?'
jokes.setup: Selina is dismissing Joe Thornhill, the ex-baseball manager running for president.
jokes.punchline: She mangles 'one-trick pony' into 'one-dick pony' — either deliberately crude or a revealing Freudian slip.
jokes.explanation: A quick, functional Selina-ism. The substitution of 'dick' for 'trick' is crude but delivered with her characteristic breezy authority, which elevates it. The joke is really about how naturally profane she is even in a dismissive aside.

jokes.text: Ben on Thornhill: 'You know that saying, "Anyone can run for president"? Joe Thornhill is the only reason that that's utter bullshit.'
jokes.setup: Ben sets up a familiar American democratic platitude about presidential candidacy.
jokes.punchline: He inverts it: Thornhill doesn't validate the saying, he single-handedly disproves it.
jokes.explanation: Tightly structured inversion of a platitude. Ben's world-weariness makes this land — he's not excited to call out Thornhill's incompetence, he's just stating a depressing fact. The structure (setup the saying, then flip it) is executed with perfect economy. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Dan's induction: 'She's gonna ask you for a chamomile. She doesn't want chamomile. She wants peppermint. She gets a little confused, but do not point it out.'
jokes.setup: Dan is delivering a high-stakes induction speech about serving the Vice President of the United States
jokes.punchline: The first and most important operational detail is that she can't correctly order her own tea
jokes.explanation: Reveals Selina's helplessness through the absurd specificity of tea confusion. The comedy is in the discrepancy between the importance of the office and the pettiness of the briefing. Character-specific and functional.

jokes.text: Thornhill: 'The United States is a team, and I wanna be your designated hitter.'
jokes.setup: Thornhill uses a baseball metaphor to describe wanting to be president.
jokes.punchline: The designated hitter has no fielding role — it's exclusively an offensive position with no defensive responsibility, which is an accidentally perfect and damning metaphor for his candidacy.
jokes.explanation: Works on two levels: Thornhill's cluelessness in using baseball metaphors for politics is funny on its face. But the baseball-literate viewer gets a bonus joke — the DH is the laziest, most one-dimensional role in baseball. The character specificity (ex-baseball manager) makes this feel earned.

jokes.text: Selina recoils from someone's Icy Hot application: 'My nostrils feel like Vietnam.'
jokes.setup: Mike has applied Icy Hot to his shoulder and the smell is overwhelming everyone in a public setting.
jokes.punchline: Selina describes her olfactory experience as 'Vietnam' — a wildly disproportionate historical reference for a muscle cream smell.
jokes.explanation: The overstatement is perfectly calibrated. 'Vietnam' as a smell metaphor is unexpected and specific — it implies chemical weapons, napalm, sensory horror. Selina's casual deployment of a war trauma reference to describe an inconvenience is very much in her character. Quotable and clean.

jokes.text: Selina losing track of her staff: 'Where is Gary? Where's everyone running off to? Is this the beginning of the Rapture or what?'
jokes.setup: Staff are scattering after the launch event for various reasons.
jokes.punchline: Selina invokes the Christian Rapture — the sudden, unexplained disappearance of the righteous — as the only explanation she can think of for people leaving her vicinity.
jokes.explanation: Functional Selina hyperbole. The Rapture reference is unexpected enough to get a laugh but doesn't have the precision of her best lines. Slightly bloated context reduces impact.

jokes.text: Ben's fantasy of speaking his mind: 'Mississippi is chock-full of assholes.' / 'I don't trust the Chinese.' / 'I'm not gonna be able to pass a single piece of legislation that's really gonna make any fuck of a difference in your life.'
jokes.setup: Ben envies Thornhill's 'speak my mind' political persona and imagines what he would say if unconstrained.
jokes.punchline: His escalating list of 'honest' political truths peaks not at a personal bigotry but at the bleakly accurate admission that legislation rarely helps anyone — which is the most damning and funniest of the three.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's first genuinely great joke. The escalation structure is masterful: the first two 'honest' statements are offensive but expected; the third pivots to systemic hopelessness, which lands harder than any bigotry could. Ben's cynical nihilism about governance is perfectly on-character, and the final line ('make any fuck of a difference') is the kind of dark political truth that makes Veep essential. Above-8 justified.

jokes.text: Ben: 'Got my vote.' Dan: 'Yeah, I got your vote.' (on Ben's fantasy platform)
jokes.setup: Ben has just described his imagined candid political platform, including nihilistic admissions.
jokes.punchline: Dan endorses it without hesitation, suggesting the honesty is genuinely appealing — a quick, dry beat.
jokes.explanation: A quick topper that works as a button to Ben's rant. Not a standalone joke but functions as a grace note. Economy is its only real virtue.

jokes.text: Selina on Ericsson: 'He's Amy without a conscience and he's Dan without the... the five percent that needs to be loved.'
jokes.setup: Selina is pitching Ericsson as the ideal campaign manager to Gary.
jokes.punchline: She reduces both her existing staff to their core psychological defects: Amy is ruthless but has a conscience; Dan is a sociopath who still needs external validation — and the pause before 'five percent' suggests she had to search for ANY human trait in Dan.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best character jokes. The construction is elegant: two parallel observations about two characters, each revealing their defining flaw while simultaneously characterizing Selina's cold analytical gaze. The 'five percent that needs to be loved' is a perfect, precise distillation of Dan Egan across the whole series. Could only come from Selina, and could only be about Dan and Amy. Justifies the 8.5 craft score.

jokes.text: Selina to Sue: 'What time is the "unofficial" lunch that I am not having today?' Sue: 'Yeah, it is not officially at 12:45.'
jokes.setup: Selina is secretly meeting a campaign manager candidate and wants plausible deniability, asking Sue about an event she officially hasn't scheduled.
jokes.punchline: Sue mirrors Selina's deniability-speak back to her with perfect robotic literalism: 'it is not officially at 12:45.'
jokes.explanation: A lovely Sue moment. Her function throughout the series is to be a perfectly calibrated bureaucratic mirror, and here she reflects Selina's euphemistic language back without irony. The joke is entirely in Sue's deadpan commitment to the bit. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Gary is washing Icy Hot out of his shirt in the office dryer. Mike emerges from a bathroom stall with a suspicious bag. Gary: 'Is that bag full of your fluids?' Mike: 'Not the whole bag. A specimen cup full of little Mikes.'
jokes.setup: Gary catches Mike coming out of a bathroom stall with an insulated bag, looking suspicious.
jokes.punchline: Mike's correction — 'not the WHOLE bag, just a specimen cup of little Mikes' — implies the distinction matters and names his sperm 'little Mikes', which is simultaneously too much information and somehow charmingly earnest.
jokes.explanation: The correction 'not the whole bag' is the joke — the implied image it creates (a bag FULL of fluids) is worse than the reality. 'Little Mikes' is perfectly Mike: earnest, slightly pathetic, humanizing. The juxtaposition of Gary washing his Icy Hot shirt while Mike handles his IVF sample is a great visual comedy sandwich.

jokes.text: Mike: 'All my "troops" are in a cold bag now, okay? I just gotta get them to the doctor so he can begin the assault on "Egg Mountain."'
jokes.setup: Mike is explaining his IVF logistics to Gary after being caught.
jokes.punchline: He frames IVF as a military campaign: 'troops' (sperm) must assault 'Egg Mountain.' The quotation marks Mike presumably makes around these terms as he says them make it more awkward.
jokes.explanation: 'Egg Mountain' is the standout image here — Mike's need to euphemize his own reproductive biology through military metaphor reveals everything about his character. The consistency of the metaphor (troops, cold bag, assault) shows craft. Very quotable and specific to Mike.

jokes.text: Amy on Dan's sudden niceness to staff: 'You guys remind me of me when I was you. Look at me now.' Ben: 'Nobody says shit like that voluntarily.'
jokes.setup: Dan is being uncharacteristically nice to junior staff as he campaigns for campaign manager.
jokes.punchline: Ben's deadpan diagnosis cuts through: no one says something that accidentally self-deprecating without an ulterior motive.
jokes.explanation: Dan's line is subtly self-incriminating ('look at me now' implies nothing's changed, they should be worried), and Ben's reaction is the real joke — his instant analytical cynicism. Solid running commentary on Dan's transparent strategizing.

jokes.text: Gary: 'Jesus, Gary, grow up.' — said TO Gary by Gary, responding to Mike's revelation
jokes.setup: Gary is processing the revelation that Mike was masturbating in the bathroom adjacent to where he was washing his shirt
jokes.punchline: Gary tells himself to 'grow up' — an absurd self-directed instruction delivered while being completely grossed out
jokes.explanation: A nice Gary moment — he references himself in the third person while lecturing himself. Character-specific and economical. The self-directed admonishment after being completely innocent in the situation is a good irony.

jokes.text: Amy on Dan as campaign manager: 'I'd rather cede control of the CIA to Pakistan than see Dan lead a campaign.'
jokes.setup: Someone asks Amy if she'd support Dan as campaign manager.
jokes.punchline: She escalates to geopolitical catastrophe as preferable — ceding CIA control to Pakistan.
jokes.explanation: Clean hyperbole with the right political specificity. 'Pakistan' is a funnier choice than a generic rival — it implies real-world geopolitical chaos. Amy's antipathy toward Dan is always funnier when it comes from a place of genuine strategic alarm rather than personal pique.

jokes.text: Amy tries to use Dan's 'be nice' strategy but admits: 'I can't do the "be nice to people" because that's what Dan's doing. Also, 'cause I can't do it.'
jokes.setup: Amy is considering how to compete with Dan for campaign manager.
jokes.punchline: She dismisses the niceness strategy first on competitive grounds, then reveals the real truth: she's physiologically incapable of it.
jokes.explanation: Amy's self-awareness about her own abrasiveness is consistently funny because she states it so matter-of-factly. The two-part reason structure (strategic, then personal) and the rhythm of 'also, 'cause I can't do it' is economically perfect.

jokes.text: Mike returns from the bathroom and claims he was 'focusing' on Selina's needs in there. Selina: 'I hope you were focusing on me in there.' Mike: 'Oh, no. I wasn't.'
jokes.setup: Selina asks where Mike has been, assuming he was thinking about her communications strategy.
jokes.punchline: Mike confirms he was absolutely not thinking about her while in the bathroom — with the audience knowing he was producing an IVF sample, the honesty is both innocent and horrifying.
jokes.explanation: The dramatic irony is doing the heavy lifting: the audience knows exactly what Mike was doing, which makes his honest 'Oh, no. I wasn't' a perfect cringe beat. Selina's obliviousness is the punchline.

jokes.text: Amy to Dan: 'You know, Dan, watching you try to be nice is like watching a baby smoke a cigarette... it's kind of cool, but also very disturbing.'
jokes.setup: Amy has been watching Dan perform niceness and finds it jarring.
jokes.punchline: The baby-smoking-a-cigarette simile is specific enough to create a vivid mental image and honest enough ('kind of cool') to avoid being a simple insult.
jokes.explanation: The episode's single best joke. The simile does something rare: it admits the thing being criticized has a certain appeal ('kind of cool') before landing on the horror. A simple insult would say 'watching Dan be nice is disturbing.' This version creates a complete, contradictory emotional response — you can imagine the image, you can see why it would be both fascinating and wrong. The construction is airtight. Highly quotable, entirely Amy's voice, could not be said by any other character on any other show. Clear 9-level work.

jokes.text: Ericsson to Selina: 'Let me guess... you're ordering dessert first and then a main course... You put yourself up for nomination before appointing a campaign manager.'
jokes.setup: Ericsson has just arrived and is already calibrating Selina's strategic incompetence.
jokes.punchline: He uses a dining metaphor for her backwards political strategy — the dessert-before-main-course analogy is elegant and slightly condescending.
jokes.explanation: A solid entry-level Ericsson joke establishing his personality as someone who arrives with critiques pre-loaded. The metaphor is apt but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Selina to Ericsson: 'That was quite a long journey for that joke. I admire your stamina.' Ericsson: 'And I admire yours. Those polls can't be easy reading.'
jokes.setup: Selina tries to defuse Ericsson's criticism with a meta-joke about his long-winded metaphor.
jokes.punchline: Ericsson turns her compliment-as-deflection around immediately, redirecting to her polling numbers — he won't be charmed.
jokes.explanation: A nice sparring exchange. Selina's meta-joke about joke length is a familiar political charm move; Ericsson's counter is faster and sharper. The dynamic — Selina trying to deploy charm, being outmaneuvered — is good character work.

jokes.text: Ericsson: 'And I admire yours. Those polls can't be easy reading. You're 30 yards ahead when you should be three miles ahead.'
jokes.setup: Selina compliments his stamina sarcastically; Ericsson returns the compliment and pivots to brutal polling reality
jokes.punchline: The yards/miles metaphor for her polling deficit — presented with total calm
jokes.explanation: Functional political insult using measurement differential. Not especially original but delivered well. Sets up the 'three-mile candidate' callback later.

jokes.text: Ericsson on Mike: 'The answer is Mike McClintock.' Selina: 'Well, that makes sense, yeah.' Ericsson on Dan: 'He's all eyelashes and teeth. He'd make a good croupier.'
jokes.setup: Ericsson is diagnosing why the press doesn't like Selina, blaming her team.
jokes.punchline: Calling Dan a 'croupier' — a casino card dealer — reduces his slick political persona to the only other place his particular brand of charm would be appropriate: extracting money from marks.
jokes.explanation: 'Croupier' is a precise, unexpected word choice that does a lot of work — it implies Dan is slick, mercenary, working in an environment where the house always wins, and is good at reading people for manipulation. Selina immediately agreeing ('He would make a good croupier') is the button.

jokes.text: Ericsson on Dan: 'Right, hitting on drunk divorcees, yeah. That's his level.'
jokes.setup: Selina defends Dan as 'very good at reading people.'
jokes.punchline: Ericsson immediately translates 'reading people' into its actual Dan application: targeting vulnerable women at bars.
jokes.explanation: Specific and uncharitable in the best way. 'Drunk divorcees' is precisely the right demographic to assign to Dan's skill set. Quick and clean.

jokes.text: Ericsson: 'I wouldn't let you within a mile of someone like me.' / 'Fire her, now.' Selina: 'I hadn't thought about...' Ericsson: 'Then it's time you did.'
jokes.setup: Ericsson is advising Selina to fire Amy for not knowing about this meeting.
jokes.punchline: His self-referential pomposity — 'someone like me' — and his clipped, commanding delivery make him the funniest presence in the room.
jokes.explanation: Ericsson's arrogance is his comedic engine and it runs well here. 'Someone like me' — referring to himself — is a small but effective display of self-importance. The rapid-fire interrupting dynamic adds to the rhythm.

jokes.text: Selina on Gary: 'Do you really want him standing behind you for the next eight years like an asshole?' Selina: 'Oh, but you know, he's MY asshole.' Ericsson: 'You need a better asshole.'
jokes.setup: Ericsson suggests firing Gary, Selina pushes back.
jokes.punchline: The word 'asshole' migrates from insult to possessive to a quality-control issue. Ericsson's 'you need a better asshole' operates on both levels simultaneously.
jokes.explanation: The wordplay here is genuinely clever: 'asshole' as a role (bag-carrier/fixer) versus 'asshole' as an insult blurs perfectly. 'You need a better asshole' is a line that would be incomprehensible out of context and hilarious in it. Good Veep construction.

jokes.text: Gary at the doctor. Doctor asks if his bag is 'integral to his job.' Gary: 'That's classified information.'
jokes.setup: Gary is seeing a doctor about his shoulder, which has been damaged by carrying Selina's bag.
jokes.punchline: When asked about his duties, Gary invokes national security classification — as if the contents of Selina's handbag are state secrets.
jokes.explanation: Perfect Gary joke. His entire character is built around pathological devotion to Selina — he carries her bag as if it's national service. Applying security clearance language to his handbag-carrying duties is exactly the self-delusion Gary requires to get through his day. High character integration score is fully justified.

jokes.text: Doctor: 'You need to get a new job.' Gary: 'Someone around here needs to get a new job, and it ain't me.' (chuckles to himself)
jokes.setup: The doctor tells Gary his shoulder requires job change to heal.
jokes.punchline: Gary's breezy deflection — as if the suggestion is ludicrous — followed by his self-satisfied chuckle reveals the depth of his delusion. The joke is that he is genuinely happy.
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in Gary's complete inability to consider an alternative life. His chuckle at the absurdity of getting a new job — as if the doctor is the one being ridiculous — is a very clean Gary beat.

jokes.text: Dan to an intern: 'Load faster, you asshole.' Gary: 'Gary, get your arm out of my airspace before I shoot it down.'
jokes.setup: Dan is being 'nice' to junior staff as his strategy, but reverts immediately when stressed.
jokes.punchline: The instant reversion to his natural abusive register when he thinks he's not being observed. 'My airspace' and 'shoot it down' for Gary's arm is a military threat over personal space.
jokes.explanation: 'My airspace' is the right kind of Dan grandiosity. Not the sharpest line but consistent character work showing the niceness campaign is entirely performative.

jokes.text: Maddox has hired Jonah. Selina: 'Are you kidding me? That's the update? I'm talking about Maddox and ME.'
jokes.setup: The team presents a Maddox update — that he hired Jonah.
jokes.punchline: Selina's fury is directed not at the political implications but at the misuse of her attention. She only cares about herself.
jokes.explanation: Functional Selina self-centeredness beat. Good character consistency but not a particularly original iteration of the joke.

jokes.text: Selina: 'I should be the "three-mile candidate."' Dan: '"Three Mile" has negative connotations.' Selina: 'Yeah. Well, there might be one here too.'
jokes.setup: Selina wants to be known as the 'three miles ahead' candidate and coins 'Three Mile Island' as a nickname without realizing the nuclear disaster association.
jokes.punchline: When Dan points out the negative connotations, Selina concedes there might be a nuclear disaster in her campaign too — accidentally self-deprecating.
jokes.explanation: The self-awareness in Selina's final line — 'there might be one here too' — is unexpected and better than a simple denial. Neatly structured, pays off well.

jokes.text: Selina on going to the countryside: 'Peeing in a bush, being talked to on a porch. It's kind of like being a dog.'
jokes.setup: Selina learns she'll be going to Maddox's country estate.
jokes.punchline: Her two-item summary of rural life — bush-peeing and porch conversation — reduces country living to canine experience.
jokes.explanation: Vivid and specific. 'Being talked to on a porch' as a canine experience is the stronger half — the passivity of it, the being-positioned-there. Good economy.

jokes.text: Mike claims to have 'really important stuff to do' and can't go to the country. Then admits he could 'do it in the country.' Then admits he has allergies.
jokes.setup: Selina invites Mike to accompany her to Maddox's country house for an important political meeting.
jokes.punchline: Mike's three-stage retreat — important duties, then 'I guess I could do it in the country,' then allergies as a real concern — reveals that he has no actual pressing work, his excuses collapse in real time.
jokes.explanation: A nice escalating incompetence sequence for Mike. The allergies as a real concern after his elaborate retreat is the right final note — his priorities are completely inverted.

jokes.text: Amy and Dan compete over who can put aside campaign manager ambitions. Dan: 'I can totally put that aside.' Amy: 'Of course.' Both immediately try to grab the spot when Selina says 'Dan, you're coming.' Amy: 'Well, actually, I have a load of...' Dan: 'Laundry to do?'
jokes.setup: Both Dan and Amy claim they can put aside their campaign manager ambitions and won't be biased.
jokes.punchline: The instant Amy loses the spot, she manufactures a conflict — 'I have a load of...' — and Dan preemptively completes it with the most embarrassing possible ending: 'Laundry to do?'
jokes.explanation: Dan's preemptive 'laundry to do?' is the perfect move — he strips away Amy's dignity before she can even manufacture her excuse. The speed of it makes it feel improvised but sharp. Strong Dan/Amy dynamic work.

jokes.text: Selina in the car on the way to Maddox's: 'People like Maddox always have guns on the walls next to the animal heads. It's like a flowchart for people who don't know what firearms do.'
jokes.setup: Selina is anticipating Maddox's country house decor as a type she recognizes.
jokes.punchline: 'A flowchart for people who don't know what firearms do' — the hunting trophies explain to the dim that guns kill animals, which is apparently necessary information.
jokes.explanation: A brilliant observational joke. 'Flowchart for people who don't know what firearms do' reframes hunting trophies as instructional diagrams for idiots — it implies the guns-plus-heads combination is redundantly explaining its own function to a cognitively limited audience. Sharp, specific, and entirely Selina's voice (educated contempt for a certain class of Republican masculinity). High quotability — this would circulate.

jokes.text: Maddox's aide Jake is introduced. Dan recognizes him as a former colleague: 'You're Maddox's bagman? That's quite a demotion.' Jake: 'I have several responsibilities for Secretary Maddox.' Dan: 'And I think you'll find I'm quite the Renaissance man.' / 'Oh, well... please, by all means, "Leonardo." Yes. Chop-chop.'
jokes.setup: Dan recognizes a former colleague who is now serving as Maddox's bagman.
jokes.punchline: Jake's defensive 'Renaissance man' self-description prompts Dan's instant mock-grandiose nickname 'Leonardo' — deployed with the same condescension he then uses to dismiss him ('chop-chop').
jokes.explanation: 'Leonardo' as a sarcastically bestowed nickname is quick and sharp — Dan granting Jake a grand historical name while literally sending him for bags is a clean power move. The 'chop-chop' is the right dismissive button.

jokes.text: Maddox: 'No, they were pre-killed.' (re: the hunting trophies)
jokes.setup: Selina asks if Maddox killed the animals on his walls
jokes.punchline: 'Pre-killed' — a bureaucratic, passive-voiced euphemism for 'someone else killed them,' delivered with complete straightness
jokes.explanation: 'Pre-killed' is one word that does everything. It's a perfect example of political language applied to embarrassing personal circumstances — the passive voice as self-protection. High economy (one word is the whole joke) and genuinely original coinage. Quotable and memorable.

jokes.text: Maddox: 'I want to thank you for agreeing to come and go fishing with me.' Selina: 'I can't wait to get out on the water. What kind of boat do you have?' Maddox: 'I don't have a boat.'
jokes.setup: Selina enthusiastically anticipates the fishing trip, imagining a boat.
jokes.punchline: No boat. River fishing. The flatness of Maddox's correction deflates Selina's nautical romanticism.
jokes.explanation: A clean anti-climax. Three words ('I don't have') puncture Selina's whole setup. The economy of the punchline is its main virtue.

jokes.text: Gary to Maddox while fishing: 'That's a nice cast... right out into the center of the water.' Selina (under her breath): 'Shut up.'
jokes.setup: Gary is trying to make small talk during the delicate fishing/political negotiation with Maddox.
jokes.punchline: His observation about a cast going into 'the center of the water' is so spectacularly vacuous — where else would a cast go? — that Selina's instant 'shut up' is pure instinct.
jokes.explanation: Gary's sycophantic compliment is the worst possible diplomatic contribution because it's also completely empty — it's praising a cast for landing in water. 'Shut up' as an immediate response, delivered in Selina's tight stage-whisper during a sensitive meeting, is very funny. Pure Gary-ness.

jokes.text: Gary: 'I'd hate to be a fish in your river.' Selina: 'Shut up.'
jokes.setup: Gary is continuing to provide running commentary on Maddox's fishing during the diplomatic meeting.
jokes.punchline: Another banal fishing-enthusiasm line ('I'd hate to be a fish in your river') gets another immediate 'shut up.'
jokes.explanation: Running gag escalation — same structure as #39 but slightly diminishing returns. Still functional and Gary's earnestness makes it land.

jokes.text: Selina begins her State Department pitch to Maddox mid-cast. He gets a fish on the line. 'Ooh! Oh, look at that! Wow, that is heavy! Reel him in!' fish escapes. Maddox: 'You fucking idiot.'
jokes.setup: Selina is in the middle of her carefully prepared political offer to Maddox (State Department) when a fish bites.
jokes.punchline: The fish escapes — presumably because of some fumble — and Maddox snaps 'You fucking idiot,' which is both the natural conclusion of the chaos and a funny violation of political diplomatic norms.
jokes.explanation: The escalation from diplomatic negotiation to fishing excitement to sudden profanity is a good Veep gear-shift. Maddox's 'you fucking idiot' applied to a VP during fishing is funny in context — it punctures the formal political register entirely.

jokes.text: Amy's dinner party: wine spill — Mike does something to cause the spill, then: 'Don't rub it, Mike. I'm making it worse.' Ben: 'I was gonna get a new rug, so... I could take a dump on it, if you need a clincher.'
jokes.setup: Mike has spilled wine on Amy's rug at her dinner party.
jokes.punchline: Ben offers to defecate on the rug to provide Amy with an excuse to replace it, treating his bodily functions as a casual social service.
jokes.explanation: Ben's offer is delivered with exactly the same tone he'd use to offer to pick up the check. The casual normality of it — as a form of social favor — is what makes it land. 'If you need a clincher' implies he's done this before. Very Ben.

jokes.text: Amy's dinner: 'Oh, there was a seating plan. But it is absolutely fine.' (after someone ignores it) / Wine is spilled on Mike, Amy: 'Don't rub it, Mike. I'm making it worse.'
jokes.setup: Amy has carefully organized a dinner to impress the senior staff and campaign for campaign manager
jokes.punchline: Everything immediately starts going wrong — the seating plan is ignored, wine is spilled
jokes.explanation: Standard cringe setup — an ambitious social event unraveling. Works because we know Amy's stakes. 'It is absolutely fine' while clearly not being fine is a good Amy beat. Not exceptional.

jokes.text: Kent to Amy, while identifying a wine by smell: 'And she has got a real nose for wine. You remember that soiree at the French embassy, where you were identifying the year by the smell?' Amy: 'I also do that with my men.'
jokes.setup: Kent is complimenting Amy's wine knowledge in an earnest, somewhat stilted way.
jokes.punchline: Amy's left-turn: she applies the same sensory connoisseurship to dating men — implying she can smell a man's vintage, quality, and possibly decay.
jokes.explanation: A clean, sharp Amy joke. The turn from wine-sniffing to male-sniffing implies her relationship history has a distinctly evaluative, almost mercenary quality — she can smell a loser. Kent's slightly stunned follow-up ('The only thing about you that doesn't surprise me is your capacity to surprise me') is a perfect, flustered button. High quotability.

jokes.text: Kent: 'The only thing about you that doesn't surprise me is your capacity to surprise me.'
jokes.setup: Amy has just said she identifies men by smell like wine vintages.
jokes.punchline: Kent's response is so syntactically careful that it becomes a joke about his inability to say anything directly — he can only describe his relationship to his own expectations.
jokes.explanation: This line sounds like it came from a compliment generator designed for someone who processes human interaction through data models. It's a perfect Kent line — technically correct, utterly inert as a social response, yet weirdly endearing.

jokes.text: Amy checks on her dinner: 'What is it?' Cook: 'Potatoes.' Amy: 'I can see that. Potatoes a la what?' Cook: 'Just potatoes.' Amy: 'You can't really eat them by themselves.' Cook: 'I've eaten hummus with a pen cap. Don't tell me how I can eat.'
jokes.setup: Amy is horrified that her dinner party's main course might be plain potatoes.
jokes.punchline: 'I've eaten hummus with a pen cap. Don't tell me how I can eat.' — a defensive non-sequitur that escalates into a strange, slightly tragic admission of subsistence eating.
jokes.explanation: A surprise stand-alone joke — the cook's response comes from nowhere and is perfect. 'Hummus with a pen cap' is hyper-specific in a way that makes it feel true (and therefore more absurd), and 'don't tell me how I can eat' applies the logic of personal dignity to the most undignified possible eating scenario. Very high quotability. One of the episode's best non-lead-character moments.

jokes.text: Kent looks at the bowl of potatoes: 'That is an ark of potato.' Amy: '"S.S. Starch."'
jokes.setup: A large bowl of potatoes is presented as the dinner party's main dish.
jokes.punchline: Kent's biblical scale reference ('an ark') is matched by Amy's ironic ship-name for it: 'S.S. Starch.'
jokes.explanation: 'Ark of potato' is a delightful Kent-ism — grandiose, precise, useless. 'S.S. Starch' is Amy's instant wit, naming the vessel. The exchange has good comedic ping-pong rhythm and both names are individually quotable.

jokes.text: Kent: 'So, Amy, when are you going to ask us to support you as campaign manager?' Amy: 'I guess I don't have to now.' She proposes a 'work' drinking game. Ben immediately says 'Work.' and drinks.
jokes.setup: Amy tries to convert her dinner into a soft political play for campaign manager support, then deflects with a drinking game rule: anyone who says 'work' drinks.
jokes.punchline: Ben says 'work' immediately and unrepentantly, drinking without a hint of guilt — he'd rather drink than pretend they're not there for political reasons.
jokes.explanation: Ben's instant 'work' is a delightful beat — he's 60 seconds ahead of everyone else's cynicism. The game collapses before it starts. Pure Ben character work.

jokes.text: Amy: 'Anyone who talks about work tonight has to take a drink.' Then immediately: 'Work.' Ben takes a drink immediately.
jokes.setup: Amy tries to set a social rule to make the dinner feel non-strategic
jokes.punchline: Ben immediately says 'Work' to drink — he'd rather drink than pretend not to be working
jokes.explanation: Ben's immediate game-breaking for the sake of drinking is perfect character comedy. He has no interest in the social pretense and would rather manipulate the rules to get drunk. Fast and economical.

jokes.text: Selina to Gary in private: 'Can I be a complete fucking bitch?' Gary: 'Please don't talk about yourself that way.'
jokes.setup: Selina is asking Gary for an honest assessment of whether she has the ruthlessness to fire her team.
jokes.punchline: Gary hears only the self-deprecating framing and instinctively leaps to defend her from the epithet — not realizing she's asking for permission to be one.
jokes.explanation: A near-perfect Gary/Selina joke. The miscommunication is built on the most fundamental Gary trait: his instinct to protect Selina from any negativity, even self-delivered. He hears 'complete fucking bitch' and his first response is concern for HER feelings, not recognition that she's asking a strategic question. The character integration here is exceptional — this joke could ONLY happen between these two specific characters. Justifies the high craft score.

jokes.text: Gary accidentally reveals Mike's IVF secret to Selina. Selina's reaction: 'I can't have Mike getting "what's-her-ass" all knocked up and pregnant, becoming some sort of a dad during my campaign.'
jokes.setup: Gary reveals Mike has been masturbating at work for IVF purposes.
jokes.punchline: Selina's objection is not about Mike's dignity or privacy, but about the inconvenience of him becoming a father during HER campaign. 'What's-her-ass' for Wendy (Mike's wife) encapsulates her total disregard for anyone's name or existence outside her campaign.
jokes.explanation: 'What's-her-ass' for Wendy is Selina at her most characteristically contemptuous — she's not bothering to remember Mike's wife's name because Wendy is not relevant to Selina's world. The pivot to 'becoming some sort of a dad during MY campaign' frames fatherhood as a scheduling inconvenience. Perfect Selina register.

jokes.text: Ben taunts Mike at dinner: 'Hey, Mike, this salad doesn't have any dressing. Did you bring any extra dressing?' then: 'I hear the next course is gonna be pulled pork... pork that has been pulled.'
jokes.setup: Everyone at Amy's dinner now knows Mike was masturbating into a cup. Ben begins making pointed food references.
jokes.punchline: 'Pork that has been pulled' — a completely unnecessary clarification that is only funny because of what everyone knows Mike was just doing.
jokes.explanation: Ben's gleeful cruelty in directing food innuendo at Mike is very much in character. 'Pork that has been pulled' as an innocent clarification is a nice bit of plausible deniability that barely holds. The dressing setup is weaker; the pulled pork payoff is sharper.

jokes.text: Mike: 'So everybody knows that I was in there masturbating into a cup now?' Gary: 'No, I was the only one who knew, Mike.' Everyone: 'We all know now.'
jokes.setup: Mike finally addresses the elephant in the room about what everyone knows.
jokes.punchline: Gary's reassurance that only he knew, combined with the group's simultaneous confirmation that they all now know, reveals Gary accidentally broadcast the information in trying to reassure Mike.
jokes.explanation: Tightly structured. Gary's reassurance ('I was the only one who knew') is immediately and collectively corrected by the room — implicating Gary as the leak without a single accusation. The economy and timing here are excellent. Good group comedy with clean cause-and-effect revelation.

jokes.text: Ben: 'Good luck with that. That can be a tough road. I have a sister who was... well, she didn't have the... it kind of all went wrong within the tubing...' Everyone: 'Stop talking.' Ben: 'Copy that.'
jokes.setup: Ben begins to offer a personal story of a relative's fertility struggles in sympathy with Mike.
jokes.punchline: His medical description deteriorates ('within the tubing') and is instantly shut down. 'Copy that' — military/professional jargon — as his response to being told to stop reveals how completely Ben operates in a transactional register even during intimate moments.
jokes.explanation: 'Within the tubing' is an excellent medical non-description — vague enough to be useless but specific enough to be horrifying. 'Copy that' as a response to 'stop talking' is the character-comedy gem here: Ben is constitutionally incapable of emotional registers.

jokes.text: Amy's toast: 'Thank you for your skills and for your friendship.' Kent: 'Hear, hear.' All: 'To skills.' / 'And friendship.'
jokes.setup: Amy gives a heartfelt toast to try to rescue the increasingly disastrous dinner
jokes.punchline: The group toasts 'to skills' first, as though professionalism genuinely comes before friendship — the order reveals what these people actually value
jokes.explanation: Subtle irony — 'to skills' before 'friendship' in a political office is a nice touch. Not a big joke but a good tonal beat.

jokes.text: Awkward pause at the dinner table. Someone: 'Um, what if we don't have anything to talk about but work?' Then someone else immediately says 'Oop... work.'
jokes.setup: The drinking game rule is 'say work, drink.' The group has exhausted non-work topics.
jokes.punchline: The meta-admission 'what if we don't have anything to talk about but work' itself contains the word 'work' — triggering the rule they were using to avoid work.
jokes.explanation: A nice structural joke — the rules of their own game catch the person trying to acknowledge the rules' failure. Good sitcom construction. The awkward silence beat that prompts the line is also part of the joke.

jokes.text: The Jonah/Jeff Kane revelation at Amy's dinner party. Group discovers Jonah's uncle is Jeff Kane (controls New Hampshire senior vote). The political dinner immediately becomes a campaign war room.
jokes.setup: Amy has set up a dinner specifically NOT to talk about work.
jokes.punchline: Within minutes, the political revelation makes the dinner entirely about work — the pretense of a social evening collapses instantly.
jokes.explanation: More plot mechanics than pure comedy, but the irony of the anti-work dinner becoming pure work is acknowledged and functional. Not a standout moment.

jokes.text: Amy kicks everyone out of her dinner party. Kent: 'Please do support Amy as Selina's campaign manager.' Amy: 'Please do. Yay me. Yay me.' Then: 'Anybody wanna get a burger?' Ben: 'Yeah.'
jokes.setup: Amy's campaign-manager-lobbying dinner has devolved into a political crisis room, and she's ejecting everyone.
jokes.punchline: Kent's earnest endorsement of Amy in front of everyone, Amy's deflated 'yay me, yay me,' and then the group immediately pivoting to burgers reveals the dinner was a complete failure on all fronts.
jokes.explanation: 'Yay me. Yay me.' is a perfect exhausted self-endorsement. The burger pivot is the right deflation — they were never invested in Amy's dinner as a social event. Good scene close.

jokes.text: Amy's boyfriend reveals it's their anniversary. Amy: 'Oh, please do not tell me you are one of those fucking weirdos that celebrates three month-a-versaries.' He says it's actually a year. Amy: 'A year in which you neglected to tell me about Jeff Kane. Go.'
jokes.setup: Amy's boyfriend reveals they have an anniversary on the same night she's kicked everyone out.
jokes.punchline: She reframes the anniversary as a year of political negligence — his failure to mention Jeff Kane's connection to Jonah is the actual crime of the relationship. 'Go.'
jokes.explanation: A top-tier Amy joke. The complete subordination of her romantic relationship to her political intelligence needs is terrifying and completely on-brand. 'A year in which you neglected to tell me about Jeff Kane' as an anniversary grievance is shocking and specific. 'Go.' — one word dismissal — is the perfect economy button. Very high character integration: this joke is entirely about who Amy is.

jokes.text: Boyfriend: 'I guess I'll go tell the violinist he has the night off.' Amy: 'Okay.' He: 'I'll have him play me a bunch of Smiths covers in my car.'
jokes.setup: Amy's boyfriend has been dumped for the evening (and possibly the relationship) after revealing he had planned a romantic anniversary evening.
jokes.punchline: He announces he'll have the violinist play him Smiths covers — a band whose catalogue is almost entirely about depression, loneliness, and romantic failure — while he sits in his car.
jokes.explanation: Specifically brilliant in its music choice. The Smiths covers-in-a-car image is vivid and deeply pathetic — and Amy's complete non-reaction ('Okay.') makes it worse. The boyfriend has accepted his fate with such melancholy specificity that the joke earns genuine sympathy alongside the laugh. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Selina learns Jonah is Maddox's entourage. Selina: 'Tell Dan he needs to find Jonah now and poach him.' Dan and Gary: 'What?' Group: 'Jeff Kane is Jonah's uncle?' Dan: 'Oh, no!'
jokes.setup: The revelation about Jonah's uncle lands for Dan and Gary for the first time.
jokes.punchline: Dan's 'Oh, no!' — realizing he has to try to recruit Jonah — is a perfectly calibrated horror response.
jokes.explanation: Dan's 'Oh, no!' is a good reaction beat — the horror of having to be nice to Jonah radiates from two words. Functional but not a standout joke.

jokes.text: Dan tries to make peace with Jonah. Jonah: 'Why the fuck do you care? Because you hate me.' Dan: 'All right, look, there's a fine line, Jonah, between hate and non-hate.'
jokes.setup: Dan is trying to charm Jonah into defecting to Selina's campaign, against his every instinct.
jokes.punchline: 'There's a fine line between hate and non-hate' — the most minimally generous possible characterization of their relationship. Dan can only just barely move Jonah from the 'hate' to the 'non-hate' column.
jokes.explanation: Brilliant in its precision. 'Hate and non-hate' is not 'like' or 'respect' — it's the minimum possible positive descriptor. Dan cannot bring himself to claim any affection; the best he can offer is the absence of documented hatred. This is a joke that works entirely through its exact word choices and reveals everything about how both characters feel.

jokes.text: Dan: 'You attacked me with a burrito.' Jonah: 'And if I could turn back the hands of time, I would.' Dan: 'Oh, what, so you could do it again?' Jonah: 'No... The juice could have gotten in my eyes, Dan. The spicy burrito juice.'
jokes.setup: Dan brings up the burrito attack as a grievance. Jonah's 'if I could turn back time' sounds like remorse.
jokes.punchline: Jonah would NOT do it again — not out of regret for Dan, but because the spicy burrito juice could have gotten in HIS OWN EYES. His concern is entirely for himself as the attacker.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes, possibly the best pure construction. The misdirection is perfect: 'I would turn back time' promises remorse. Dan's suspicious interpretation ('so you could do it again?') is correct instinct. But the real answer is neither — Jonah regrets it because HE HIMSELF could have been hurt. 'The spicy burrito juice' as repeated with total gravity is a perfect detail. The structure is textbook: setup → apparent subversion → deeper, more specific subversion. Very high marks across all craft dimensions, fully justified.

jokes.text: Dan: 'You attacked me with a burrito.' / 'And if I could turn back the hands of time, I would.' / Jonah: 'Oh, what, so you could do it again?' / Dan: 'No. The juice could have gotten in my eyes, Dan... the spicy burrito juice.'
jokes.setup: Dan is apologizing for a past burrito attack to win Jonah over
jokes.punchline: Dan's regret is not for the act of violence but for the personal danger — the spicy juice near his eyes — and he accidentally calls himself 'Dan' instead of Jonah
jokes.explanation: 'The spicy burrito juice' is the standout. Dan's apology reveals his only regret was self-endangerment, which tells us everything about him. The Jonah/Dan slip is a nice bonus detail. The escalation from 'apology' to 'burrito juice eye protection concern' is perfectly structured. Strong 8+ case.

jokes.text: Jonah on his own desirability: 'People like me, people hire me, people date me, and people fuck me because I'm triple-A fuckin' awesome and no other reason.'
jokes.setup: Dan implies Jonah only has his job because of his uncle, Jeff Kane.
jokes.punchline: Jonah's furious, grandiose self-defense is a perfect inversion of his actual standing — the comedy is that literally none of this is true and the audience knows it.
jokes.explanation: The escalation through 'like,' 'hire,' 'date,' and 'fuck' to arrive at 'triple-A fuckin' awesome' is perfect Jonah — his self-image is so disconnected from consensus reality that it's almost admirable. The series context makes this doubly funny: Jonah is one of the most universally despised characters on the show.

jokes.text: Jonah: 'Why don't you scooch your little butt out of my fuckin' pantry?' / 'You know what? The only reason you have a fuckin' job is because your uncle is "Donald Chump." Wake up and smell the fuckin' burrito juice.'
jokes.setup: Dan won't leave despite being rejected. Jonah tries to eject him while also firing back about Dan's connections.
jokes.punchline: 'Donald Chump' as a made-up insulting uncle figure, combined with 'wake up and smell the fuckin' burrito juice' — a riff on 'wake up and smell the coffee' but using their specific history.
jokes.explanation: 'Wake up and smell the fuckin' burrito juice' earns high marks for being both a fresh riff on a cliché AND a callback to the burrito attack in the same conversation. The burrito juice becomes a running motif within this single scene. 'Donald Chump' is a decent gag name but less original.

jokes.text: Jonah: 'You know what? Selina's gonna lose and President Maddox is gonna have you both executed!' Dan: 'That's not even the way it works, you fuckin' idiot.' Jonah: 'Oh, yeah? Well, maybe you're a fuckin' idiot.'
jokes.setup: Jonah's political threats escalate to claims about presidential executions.
jokes.punchline: Dan's incredulous correction (presidents don't execute staff) leads to Jonah's blistering counter-argument: 'maybe you're a fuckin' idiot.' The return insult is the laziest possible rebuttal, deployed with complete confidence.
jokes.explanation: 'Maybe you're a fuckin' idiot' as a final comeback to being called an idiot is perfectly Jonah: no new information, no counter-argument, just the same insult bounced back. His confidence in this non-argument is the joke.

jokes.text: Selina: 'I believe that the position of vice president can be a very rewarding one.' Maddox: 'Even though it has no power?' Selina: 'It has some constitutional power, and, by the way, I think that I can pull some pretty big levers.' Maddox: 'You exercise this power very discreetly, I must say.'
jokes.setup: Maddox is offering Selina the VP slot on his ticket. She tries to spin the position as desirable.
jokes.punchline: Maddox's 'you exercise this power very discreetly' is a devastating understated burn — she's the sitting Vice President and has accomplished nothing notable.
jokes.explanation: 'Very discreetly' as a synonym for 'you have no power and nothing to show for it' is impeccably calibrated British-style understatement. Maddox's deadpan delivery of this devastating assessment — during what Selina thinks is a negotiation — is a very strong joke. High craft scores justified by the economy, character integration, and originality of the construction.

jokes.text: Selina: 'I'd rather be shot in the fucking face than serve as vice president again. Seriously, in the fucking face!'
jokes.setup: Maddox has just offered Selina the VP position on his ticket — the same job she currently has.
jokes.punchline: Selina's response escalates immediately to graphic self-harm preference, then doubles down with 'seriously, in the fucking face' — as if face-shooting was a credibility upgrade over regular shooting.
jokes.explanation: The repetition and specification ('seriously, in the fucking face') is what elevates this above a basic 'I hate my job' joke. The callback to the entire premise of the show — Selina is defined by her hatred of the VP role — makes this a series-level character moment. The escalation of 'in the face' as a specificity that somehow indicates greater commitment to the statement is genuinely funny writing.

jokes.text: Selina returns from the country house defeated. She just moans. Dan: 'All right, guys, listen. Maddox is running.' Password is 'mother_fucker.' 'So our next move is to stop Chung from running.' Selina: 'No.'
jokes.setup: Selina returns having failed to stop Maddox. Dan immediately pivots to the next plan.
jokes.punchline: After Selina's defeated moaning, Dan's crisp pivot to 'password: mother_fucker' and next steps, followed by Selina's flat 'No' to his Chung plan, captures the complete disconnect between Dan's momentum and Selina's collapse.
jokes.explanation: 'Mother_fucker' as the Dropbox password is a small, functional joke. The scene's comedy is largely structural — defeat meeting relentless forward motion — rather than a specific standout line.

jokes.text: Dan on female candidates' electability: 'The subtext of every question will be, "Yeah, you're pretty, but can you break a man's neck?"'
jokes.setup: Dan is arguing that two male military candidates running will squeeze out Selina.
jokes.punchline: He frames the media scrutiny as essentially asking whether she can commit physical violence — reducing gender bias in presidential campaigns to its most primitive, absurdist expression.
jokes.explanation: 'Can you break a man's neck?' as the distilled question of military-adjacent political masculinity is original and precise. It's also a genuine piece of political satire — the absurdist reduction of 'commander-in-chief' qualifications to literal violence. Dan saying it so matter-of-factly makes it land. High originality justified.

jokes.text: Mike is still carrying his specimen cooler to work. Selina: 'Oh. What's in your lunchbox, Mike? A protein shake? You degenerate.' ... 'I'd like you to do me the honor of removing your jizzbox from our executive branch of government.'
jokes.setup: Selina discovers Mike still has his IVF specimen cooler in the office.
jokes.punchline: 'Jizzbox' as a descriptor, followed by 'from our executive branch of government' — a phrase that sounds like it's about to be about national policy and ends with Mike's cooler.
jokes.explanation: 'Remove your jizzbox from our executive branch of government' is an exceptional line — it layers crude vocabulary ('jizzbox') against the grandest possible institutional language ('executive branch of government') and the collision is perfect. The callback payoff for the IVF subplot peaks here. High quotability. One of the episode's best Selina lines.

jokes.text: Mike: 'It's actually a cooler.' Selina: 'Oh, then, by all means. Open it up, let's have a picnic. I'll boil up my eggs.'
jokes.setup: Mike corrects 'jizzbox' to 'cooler.'
jokes.punchline: 'I'll boil up my eggs' — Selina's picnic suggestion is innocent on its face but absolutely loaded in context, since 'eggs' in this IVF subplot rhymes directly with reproductive biology.
jokes.explanation: The 'boil my eggs' double entendre in an IVF context is perfect: Selina may or may not be aware of the implication, and the ambiguity is funnier than either interpretation. Good callback button for the IVF thread.

jokes.text: Selina's private assessment of her team: 'Desperate Dan, flailing Amy, crippled Gary, jizzy Mike.'
jokes.setup: Selina is confiding in Ericsson her plan to replace her whole team.
jokes.punchline: She reduces each staff member to a single adjective, each perfectly characterizing their current low point: 'jizzy Mike' landing as the most memorable.
jokes.explanation: Four adjectives, four characters, perfect economy. The rhythm — desperate, flailing, crippled, jizzy — escalates to the best/worst one last. 'Jizzy Mike' as a permanent epithet is the culmination of the episode's IVF running gag. Extremely quotable, beautifully economical.

jokes.text: Selina (to Ericsson): 'I'm gonna shoot Mike and Gary in the head, I'll poison Amy, I'll behead Dan.' Turns and sees the entire team standing there. 'Oh, shit. What are you doing here?'
jokes.setup: Selina is listing her plans to get rid of the team to Ericsson using violent metaphors.
jokes.punchline: The entire team she was just describing is standing right there. Selina's 'Oh, shit' is the comedy — the visual of the team listening to their own mock executions.
jokes.explanation: A classic sitcom 'caught in the act' beat, elevated by the specificity of the violent imagery used to describe firing people. The visual of the team standing there is the punchline. Functional, gets a good reaction, but the cringe comedy here is more structural than verbal.

jokes.text: Ericsson announces he's going with Thornhill instead of Selina. Selina: 'Baseball Joe? The man's a vegetable. Seriously.' Ericsson: 'He can win.' Selina: 'I don't love your attitude. So why don't you go and...' Ericsson (interrupting): 'With all due respect...' Selina: 'You know what? You stop interrupting me, you stupid fucking interrupty guy.'
jokes.setup: Ericsson, who has been repeatedly interrupting Selina throughout the episode, does it again at the worst possible moment.
jokes.punchline: 'You stupid fucking interrupty guy' — Selina's profanity-laced insult weaponizes a non-word ('interrupty') in her rage, which is simultaneously more and less sophisticated than a real insult.
jokes.explanation: 'Interrupty' is a masterclass in Selina's anger: she's so furious she invents an adjective. The word doesn't exist, which makes it funnier — her need to describe the thing is more urgent than her ability to find the correct word for it. Also a callback to the episode's running gag of Ericsson cutting her off. 'You stupid fucking interrupty guy' has become one of Veep's most memorable insults. Fully justifies 8+ craft.

jokes.text: Selina fires Ericsson: 'I am un-offering you the job.' Ericsson: 'Well, it was a pleasure to meet you, ma'am.' Selina: 'You bet it was. It was a huge pleasure to meet me.'
jokes.setup: Ericsson politely exits after Selina fires him.
jokes.punchline: 'It was a huge pleasure to meet me' — Selina interprets his politeness as endorsement of herself, converting a standard pleasantry into a testimonial.
jokes.explanation: Selina's narcissism expressed through language is a recurring joke, but 'it was a huge pleasure to meet me' is a particularly clean example. She completes Ericsson's sentence from her own perspective, as if the pleasure could only logically be about her. The word 'huge' — amplifying an already self-serving statement — is the right button. High character integration.

jokes.text: Selina asks Dan if he wants to be campaign manager. Long pause. Dan: 'Yes.' Selina: 'Yeah.' Another pause. 'Yes, I do.' 'Okay.' 'Yeah!' 'Yeah, mm!'
jokes.setup: After the entire episode of maneuvering, Selina simply asks Dan directly.
jokes.punchline: The multiple beats of confirmation — both of them unable to move past the moment — is both an anticlimactic end to all the scheming and a genuine character beat: Dan is almost tender in this moment.
jokes.explanation: The comedy here is the extended awkwardness of two people who are never sincere being almost sincere. Dan's 'Mm!' at the end — a noise of barely contained excitement — is a nice touch. The scene earns its runtime.

jokes.text: Gary: 'I feel all floaty. What's this one called?' Mike: 'It's a boilermaker.' Gary: 'A boilermaker!' (laughs) 'I'm a Prosecco man.' Mike: 'I wouldn't spread that around, Gary.'
jokes.setup: Gary and Mike are at a bar drinking. Gary has apparently never had a boilermaker.
jokes.punchline: Gary identifying as 'a Prosecco man' reveals his entire personality — delicate, slightly effete, devoted to Selina's world of female-coded taste. Mike's 'I wouldn't spread that around' is a gentle masculine social warning.
jokes.explanation: 'I'm a Prosecco man' is a perfect Gary line — he states it with genuine pride, totally oblivious to the social coding. Mike's protective warning adds a rare moment of male solidarity between them. Good character work.

jokes.text: Gary: 'I carry around Selina's big, heavy bag of her tampon bullshit.' (laughs) 'The doctor told me to get another job.' Mike: 'You need to get another doctor.'
jokes.setup: Gary has revealed his shoulder injury at the doctor, and now drunkenly admits what the doctor said.
jokes.punchline: Mike's 'you need to get another doctor' — as if the medical advice itself is the problem rather than the job — is a loyalty over logic response that mirrors Gary's own worldview.
jokes.explanation: 'Her tampon bullshit' as the contents of the sacred bag is very funny — Gary's complete devotion to Selina apparently extends to carrying her feminine hygiene products with equal dignity as state documents. Mike's 'get another doctor' is the right character beat: he can't conceive of Gary leaving either.

jokes.text: Mike pantomimes ordering a cortisone shot, then 'tequila,' then 'tequila-aa... oh...!' — implying shots for both the shoulder and the bar.
jokes.setup: Mike suggests Gary get a cortisone shot for his shoulder.
jokes.punchline: He calls for a 'cortisone shot' at the bar as a joke, then orders tequila shots to follow, performing the dual meaning of 'shot' for an appreciative bar crowd.
jokes.explanation: A serviceable bar joke on 'shot' — medical vs. alcohol. Not Veep's sharpest construction; the pun is fairly obvious. The physical performance sells it more than the writing.

jokes.text: Amy arrives at the bar and is horrified to find Mike's specimen cooler on the bar. Mike: 'Relax, Amy. It's a spoiled batch. I missed my window today at the clinic and Wendy is not happy.'
jokes.setup: Amy finds the IVF cooler on the bar top among glasses.
jokes.punchline: 'A spoiled batch' is applied to Mike's sperm sample with the same language one might use for a batch of soup. 'Wendy is not happy' implies a home front consequence to the day's IVF misadventures.
jokes.explanation: Good running gag continuation. 'A spoiled batch' in a bar context is funny for the clinical detachment it applies to reproductive biology. The IVF plot has now moved from office bathroom to car to bar — escalating its public inappropriateness.

jokes.text: Kent arrives at the bar: 'Hey, kids. Everybody well oiled?' Then announces there 'may be some changes.' Amy: 'She got a campaign manager, didn't she?' Kent: 'Please tell me it's not Dan.' It is Dan. Amy: 'Fuck you, Dan, you minor-league gigolo!'
jokes.setup: Kent arrives with news, everyone expects the worst.
jokes.punchline: Amy's immediate 'Fuck you, Dan, you minor-league gigolo!' — 'minor-league' in particular is perfectly chosen (echoing the sports theme of the episode, reducing Dan's sexual prowess to the bush leagues).
jokes.explanation: 'Minor-league gigolo' is a very good insult — it implies Dan has the right instincts but insufficient talent for the major leagues of sexual manipulation. The baseball episode context (Thornhill) makes 'minor-league' land with extra resonance. Clean and quotable.

jokes.text: Gary: 'Gigolo!' (echoing Amy's insult with delight)
jokes.setup: Amy has just called Dan a 'minor-league gigolo.'
jokes.punchline: Gary enthusiastically echoes the word 'gigolo' — apparently finding it the most exciting vocabulary he's encountered all day.
jokes.explanation: A quick Gary beat — his enthusiasm for the word says more about his sheltered life than anything. Functional topper.

jokes.text: Dan says he doesn't mind being called a gigolo because he likes older women. 'They do. They have life experience. Not just the sexy stuff. Well, they've got that too, I can tell you that.' Selina goes silent and then coughs.
jokes.setup: Dan defends his reputation as a gigolo by vouching for older women's qualities. Selina, who is older, is in the conversation.
jokes.punchline: Dan's 'they've got that too, I can tell you that' — delivered with conviction — combined with Selina's uncomfortable clearing of the throat creates a deeply awkward implied moment.
jokes.explanation: A slow-burn cringe moment that works on accumulated context. Dan's progressive narrowing of his older-women speech — 'not just the sexy stuff... well, they've got that too' — moves closer and closer to an implied claim about Selina. Her physical reaction (the cough) is the punchline — she can't address it directly, so her body does. Good structural cringe.

jokes.text: Selina and Dan begin to bond over dark secrets as the new campaign team. Dan says he has 'skeletons.' Selina: 'Oh-kay. Here we go.'
jokes.setup: Selina and Dan are starting their new working relationship and Selina invites Dan to share his skeletons.
jokes.punchline: Selina's preemptive 'Oh-kay. Here we go.' suggests she expected this and isn't sure she wants to know. The resignation in the phrasing is the joke.
jokes.explanation: A light setup beat for the skeleton-confession scene. Functional but not a joke in itself — more of a character moment that signals what's coming.

jokes.text: Dan's skeleton: 'When I was a kid... a bunch of older kids... dared me... to kill this stray dog. And I did.'
jokes.setup: Selina asks Dan for his skeletons as campaign manager. He builds to it with heavy-breathing dramatic pauses.
jokes.punchline: He killed a dog on a dare. Just... did it. The casual confirmation — 'And I did' — after all the dramatic pausing is more horrifying than any political scandal would be.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's three best jokes. The misdirection is perfect: the dramatic pauses and the political context prime the audience for a scandalous career secret. 'I killed a dog' is shocking, specific, and somehow completely consistent with everything we know about Dan Egan. 'And I did' — the flat, matter-of-fact confirmation — is a masterclass in deadpan delivery direction. It's also genuinely disturbing while remaining funny. The character integration is exceptional: this is not a generic dark-past joke, it is specifically the Dan Egan dark past. Fully justifies 8.8.

jokes.text: Selina's skeleton: She admits to setting her ex-husband Andrew's car on fire. 'I torch cars, you know... give me the nuclear codes.' (chuckles)
jokes.setup: Selina's turn to share a skeleton. She reciprocates with her own dark secret.
jokes.punchline: 'I torch cars, you know... give me the nuclear codes.' — she makes the logical leap herself: if this is who she is, she should definitely have access to weapons of mass destruction.
jokes.explanation: The episode's best joke. 'Give me the nuclear codes' as the punchline to a car-arson confession is Veep at its absolute peak. The joke works on three levels simultaneously: (1) Selina is confessing to an actual crime; (2) she's doing so while campaigning for the presidency, which comes with real nuclear weapons; (3) her response to the confession — ironic self-deprecation that is also, given her actual ambitions, a literal campaign pitch. The 'you know...' before 'give me the nuclear codes' implies she's made this connection before and found it charming. Exceptional craft. This is a 9+ joke — specific to these characters, in this episode, in this political moment, building from a perfectly constructed scene. Exceptional character integration for Selina.

jokes.text: Amy tries to call Selina from the bar to warn about the Ericsson betrayal. Dan: 'I invited you guys to dinner.' Amy: 'Yeah, then you kicked us out.'
jokes.setup: The bar group realizes what's happening: Selina chose Dan and Dan has been with Selina all night.
jokes.punchline: Amy's 'Yeah, then you kicked us out' — the technical accuracy of why she was calling Selina instead of being with her is a complete, correct, damning answer.
jokes.explanation: A functional callback and clarification beat. Not a standout joke but maintains the episode's ironic throughline — Amy's dinner was meant to build loyalty and instead destroyed it.

jokes.text: Kent's plan: 'We should throw your expired cum at Jonah's door.' Group chants: 'Let's throw cum! Let's throw cum!' Mike: 'Amy, no, no... that's my DNA!'
jokes.setup: The group is drunk and angry. Kent or Gary proposes using Mike's now-expired IVF specimen as a weapon against Jonah.
jokes.punchline: The group chants 'Let's throw cum' with genuine excitement. Mike's protest — 'that's my DNA!' — is both medically correct and deeply pathetic.
jokes.explanation: The IVF subplot's final, glorious culmination. The group — including Kent, the most data-driven, professorial character — chanting 'Let's throw cum' is the episode's highest-energy comedic peak. Mike's 'that's my DNA!' is both a valid objection and the saddest possible way to defend your dignity. The escalation from 'IVF sample in a bag' to 'biological weapon against Jonah' is a perfect Veep story engine. High impact score: this is the episode's biggest laugh moment.

jokes.text: Selina, back at home: 'How did you kill it?' (the dog, to Dan) Dan: 'Sorry, what?' Selina: 'Nothing. It's fine.' Pause. 'We're good?' Dan: 'Yeah.' 'All right.'
jokes.setup: After the revelations, Selina and Dan are in their new professional relationship.
jokes.punchline: Selina cannot help but ask how Dan killed the dog, then immediately walks it back — 'nothing, it's fine' — but the question was already out there. The awkward beat afterward confirms she will sit with the curiosity forever.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's very best closing jokes. The question 'how did you kill it?' comes from nowhere but is instantly recognizable as something that would haunt anyone who just heard that confession. Selina asking, then immediately pulling back — 'nothing. It's fine.' — is a perfect human impulse. The 'we're good?' beat after is loaded: are they? The dark callback earns full marks on structure and economy. This is the scene that makes the Dan dog-killing joke immortal.

jokes.text: Mike's closing talking head: 'Had a romantic dinner... champagne, oysters, then Wendy finished me off in a cup. It was beautiful.'
jokes.setup: Mike gives a closing confessional describing his evening.
jokes.punchline: 'Wendy finished me off in a cup. It was beautiful.' — Mike describes his IVF sample collection as a romantic act of partnership, complete with genuine sentiment. 'It was beautiful' applied to cup-based sperm collection is the kicker.
jokes.explanation: A spectacular episode closer for the IVF subplot. The construction — romantic dinner buildup with champagne and oysters, which are already ridiculous — climaxes in 'then Wendy finished me off in a cup.' The word 'finished' is doing enormous work: it's both clinically accurate and romantically charged. 'It was beautiful' — delivered with apparent sincerity — is the twist that makes the whole thing land. This is Mike's best moment in the episode. The confessional format gives it the gravity of a testimony. Near the top of the episode's craft hierarchy.

jokes.text: Ben: 'Sounds pretty good, except for the oysters. Yeah, well, I like them.' / Group plans to throw the specimen at Jonah's door 'like a contract killing.'
jokes.setup: Ben responds to Mike's romantic IVF story.
jokes.punchline: Ben's only objection to Mike's IVF narrative is the oysters — everything else (champagne, cup, wife) is apparently fine, just the oysters are a dealbreaker. The specificity of the objection is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Ben's purely personal, irrelevant objection to the oysters in a story about cup-mediated reproduction is a great Ben joke. He processes the world through personal preference even when everyone else is horrified. The 'like a contract killing' line for the sperm-throwing operation is a good button.

jokes.text: The group approaches Jonah's door with Mike's specimen. 'Shh, shh. We do this... where is it? ...like a contract killing.' Knocking. Hurrying. Then: 'Hey, guys.' — Jonah opens the door.
jokes.setup: The group has decided to throw Mike's expired IVF specimen at Jonah's door as revenge.
jokes.punchline: Jonah himself opens the door just as they're about to execute the plan — the perfect anticlimax that denies them the satisfying throw and confronts them with the actual person.
jokes.explanation: A brilliant final visual gag for the IVF subplot: the episode ends on the image of the entire group caught outside Jonah's door with a bag of Mike's expired sperm, confronted by Jonah's cheerful 'Hey, guys.' The comedic tension of 'what do they do NOW?' is the perfect cliffhanger that the episode wisely doesn't resolve. The episode closes on this image.

tmdb_overview: Selina has a secret lunch with a major strategist she may tap as her campaign manager, even as Amy and Dan are openly vying for the job. Meanwhile, Defense Secretary Maddox invites the vice president to his country estate; Gary gets bad news about his shoulder; and Mike's colleagues find out he's mixing business with pleasure.
insight: Escalation-driven absurdism sustains momentum despite middling 78.8 score across 54 jokes.
